skill_progression.philosophy: Use skills to improve them, or find instruction, or study. No arbitrary gates. Mastery takes years of in-game time. A farmer who picks up a sword fights like a farmer.
skill_progression.player_facing_display: Players see tier names and a qualitative sense of progress within a tier — not the raw number. The raw number drives the simulation.

tiers.name: Trace
tiers.description: You've touched it. Aware of basics. Fail less catastrophically than zero.

tiers.name: Apprentice
tiers.description: Can do simple tasks reliably. Makes beginner mistakes on complex ones.

tiers.name: Journeyman
tiers.description: Competent. Earns wages doing this. Rarely embarrasses themselves.

tiers.name: Skilled
tiers.description: Noticeably better than average. Minor regional reputation.

tiers.name: Expert
tiers.description: Sought out. People travel for their work. Years of focused effort to reach.

tiers.name: Master
tiers.description: Rare. Defining practitioner in a region. Probably has a name.

background_starting_levels.note: The gap between signature and trace skills is immediately legible and meaningful

ranges.reason: Past the basics — varied problems needed
Working at the edge of understanding
Repetition stops working — novel challenges required
Years of high-stakes varied application. Some never reach it.

progression_curve.repetition_problem: Doing the same task repeatedly has diminishing returns. The system tracks variety of application, not just quantity of use. Encountering a new problem within a skill accelerates improvement temporarily.
progression_curve.variety_bonus: A blacksmith working only nails plateaus. A blacksmith taking varied commissions — tools, weapons, mail repair, architectural ironwork — continues progressing.

advancement_vectors.name: Active Use
advancement_vectors.description: Do the thing, the skill improves. Rate follows the progression curve above.
advancement_vectors.notes: Most reliable. No cost. Always available. Slows dramatically at high tiers.

advancement_vectors.name: Formal Instruction
advancement_vectors.description: Finding a qualified teacher. Paid in coin, labor, or favor. Gives a multiplier to active use gains.
advancement_vectors.instruction_ceiling: A teacher can only accelerate progress to roughly their own level minus 50 points. A Journeyman teacher (level 175) can push a student to about 125 efficiently.
advancement_vectors.finding_instructors: Instruction is embedded in relationships and circumstances. No skill trainers with floating icons. A mercenary veteran teaches weapon skills to promising recruits. A temple priest teaches religious lore to those in service. An old blacksmith teaches a promising apprentice in exchange for forge work.

advancement_vectors.name: Study
advancement_vectors.description: Books, manuscripts, written records. Requires functional literacy (skill 20+).
what_study_can_do: Push a skill from 0 to Trace without practical experience
Provide context that makes active use 1.3x more efficient while studying
Unlock understanding of advanced techniques that can then be practiced toward
what_study_cannot_do: Replace physical training — cannot read your way to swordsmanship
Push a physical skill above ~100 without corresponding practice
Teach things the book doesn't contain — most texts are incomplete, outdated, or copied with errors
advancement_vectors.literacy_dependency: Literacy skill below 20: can puzzle through simple texts slowly with gaps. Zero literacy: can listen to someone read aloud — much slower, no retention bonus, but not zero.
advancement_vectors.book_availability: Rare outside cities and temples. The Archive of Keth has texts that push knowledge skills in ways unavailable anywhere else.

advancement_vectors.name: Observation
advancement_vectors.description: Watching a skilled practitioner without formal instruction or hands-on practice.
what_it_does: Can move a zero to Trace given enough sustained observation
Slightly accelerates progress in the Trace-to-Apprentice range
Background noise of being in skill-practicing environments
advancement_vectors.example: A character working in a blacksmith shop doing unskilled labor will pick up low-level metalworking knowledge over time whether trying to or not.

prerequisite_relationships.reason: Heavy blade requires raw power to leverage effectively
Cannot operate without baseline wound management
Compounding requires knowing what the plants do
Cannot fight from a horse you cannot control
Cannot convincingly fake what you cannot read
Celestial navigation extends spatial understanding — it is not standalone

synergy_relationships.effect: Each point in one gives 0.3x passive bonus to the other
synergy_relationships.reason: A smith who understands metal understands weapon maintenance. A meticulous maintainer learns metallurgy.

synergy_relationships.effect: Development in one gives 0.3x passive gains to the other
synergy_relationships.reason: The same plant knowledge base approached from different angles

synergy_relationships.effect: Advancing either past 150 (Journeyman) unlocks an accelerated track for the other
High Stealth gives a +50 floor bonus to Tailing (minimum effective Tailing = Stealth - 50)
synergy_relationships.reason: Cannot trail someone effectively if you cannot move quietly

synergy_relationships.effect: +25 bonus to weapon skill when fighting in a formation line
synergy_relationships.reason: Formation discipline transfers to weapon use

synergy_relationships.effect: At 226+ (Skilled) in Seamanship, Maritime Navigation gains accelerate 1.5x through active use at sea
synergy_relationships.reason: At Skilled seamanship you understand the sea well enough to read it navigationally

transfer_relationships.effect: High competence in grain gives Trace starting point in others for new characters. Fundamentals of soil and season transfer.
Shared principles of edge, guard, distance — 1.3x progression rate when learning dagger
Structural thinking transfers. A master carpenter starting masonry begins at Trace rather than zero, and progresses 1.2x
transfer_relationships.reason: Understanding how structures bear load transfers across materials
General animal knowledge provides strong foundation

physical_skill_ceilings_by_age.note: Physical skill ceilings decline with age. Knowledge and social skills have no ceiling — and gain passive bonuses in the 41–72 band.
physical_skill_ceilings_by_age.compensation_rule: High Tactics or deep weapon knowledge creates qualitative advantages against younger opponents. Does not raise the ceiling — makes fights less predictable, not necessarily winnable.

condition_modifiers.note: Performance modifiers only. Rest, eat, heal — the skill returns. Only permanent injuries leave lasting penalties.

modifiers.effect: All skills: -25. Judgment-dependent skills (Tactics, Deception, Persuasion): -50.
All skills: -100. Catastrophic failure risk on complex tasks.
Relevant skills: -75 to -200 depending on severity. A broken sword arm is effectively zero for blade work.
Starts at -25, worsens by -10 per day until treated.
Physical: -15. Some social skills: +25 (inhibition reduced). Judgment skills: -50.
Physical: -100. All cognitive: -100. Coordination failure risk.

journeyman_range.decay: Very slow. Months of non-use for any perceptible drop. Rate: 1 point per month idle.

skilled_range.decay: Slow. Rate: 1 point per week idle.

expert_range.decay: Moderate. Rate: 2 points per week idle.

master_range.decay: Active maintenance required. Rate: 3 points per week idle. Masters who stop practicing lose ground within weeks.

decay_floor.rule: A skill never decays below half its peak recorded level, rounded down.
decay_floor.example: Peak of 380 (Expert low) floors at 190 (Journeyman high). The knowledge is there. The edge is gone.
decay_floor.rebuild_rate: Re-training a decayed skill returns to peak at 2x normal progression rate. The body remembers.
